frameworks.name: dual-process signal detection
frameworks.one_line: separate mechanisms support perceptual decisions and metacognitive monitoring

frameworks.name: single-process signal detection
frameworks.one_line: one mechanism predicts perceptual and metacognitive fatigue should correlate positively

frameworks.name: limited cognitive resources / vigilance decrement
frameworks.one_line: shared finite resources allocated between perception and metacognition over time

frameworks.name: frontal polar metacognition
frameworks.one_line: prefrontal gray matter volume linked to visual metacognitive ability

core_question: Do perceptual performance and metacognitive sensitivity decline together over time, or do they trade off?
key_assumptions: metacognitive sensitivity is how well confidence tracks accuracy
vigilance decrement reflects depletion of a limited resource
frontal polar cortex contributes to metacognition
manipulations: time on task within long behavioral blocks
metacognitive demand (with vs without confidence reporting)
individual gray matter volume of frontal polar area
key_findings: perceptual and metacognitive fatigue showed negative or near-zero correlations
frontal polar gray matter volume correlated with individual behavioral differences
reducing metacognitive demand improved perceptual vigilance
interpretations: perceptual and metacognitive decisions rely on distinct mechanisms drawing on common resources
frontal polar cortex may supply shared resources arbitrated between the two processes
